_meta.description: stretch_prompts_base seed data — 10 profiles × 5 cycles × 3 positions × 3 variants

prompts.story_starter: The decision had already been made.
prompts.instruction: Continue in a voice that takes full ownership of a choice, without apology or hedge.
prompts.word_guidance: Aim for 100–130 words. Lead with position, not context.

prompts.story_starter: She had heard enough arguments.
prompts.instruction: Write the definitive response — one that closes discussion rather than opening it.
prompts.word_guidance: 100–120 words. Make every sentence a statement, not a question.

prompts.story_starter: There was only one way this would end.
prompts.instruction: Write forward from certainty. No alternative futures — just this one.

prompts.story_starter: Everyone in the room waited for him to speak.
prompts.instruction: Give the speech that changes the room. Direct, specific, no throat-clearing.
prompts.word_guidance: 120–150 words. Open with the main point.

prompts.story_starter: The report on her desk said one thing.
prompts.instruction: Write the memo that responds to it — unambiguous, action-oriented, decisive.

prompts.story_starter: He knew what had happened, even if no one else did.
prompts.instruction: Write from the perspective of someone who holds private certainty. Confident, not smug.

prompts.story_starter: The question wasn't whether it would work.
prompts.instruction: Finish the thought. Write as someone who has already moved past doubt into execution.
prompts.word_guidance: 100–130 words. Past the question, into the answer.

prompts.story_starter: Three years ago she would have asked for permission.
prompts.instruction: Show the before-and-after through action, not explanation. Show agency gained.

prompts.story_starter: The contract arrived on a Tuesday.
prompts.instruction: Write the moment of decision — swift, unambiguous, consequential.

prompts.story_starter: Nobody had asked for her opinion, but she gave it anyway.
prompts.instruction: Write in the voice of someone whose opinion has weight — not because of status, but conviction.

prompts.story_starter: The numbers told a story no one wanted to hear.
prompts.instruction: Tell it anyway. Write the paragraph that names the uncomfortable truth plainly.

prompts.story_starter: This was not the time for careful words.
prompts.instruction: Write the passage that matches urgency with precision — direct but not reckless.

prompts.story_starter: She had said it before and would say it again.
prompts.instruction: Write the repetition that gains, not loses, authority with each iteration.

prompts.story_starter: The map was wrong.
prompts.instruction: Write the correction — not an accusation, but a clear redirect toward the actual route.

prompts.story_starter: In twenty years of doing this work, he had learned one thing.
prompts.instruction: Distil that one thing. State it simply and build the case in the next two sentences.

prompts.story_starter: The problem wasn't complicated, despite what everyone said.
prompts.instruction: Simplify the complication. Cut through without being dismissive.

prompts.story_starter: What she wanted was very specific.
prompts.instruction: Name it. Specifically, concretely — no vague gesturing toward desire.

prompts.story_starter: He had stopped explaining himself years ago.
prompts.instruction: Write the scene where this shows — not as backstory, but as present-tense fact.

prompts.story_starter: The answer was no.
prompts.instruction: Write the paragraph that follows a clean refusal — firm, fair, final.

prompts.story_starter: This was the version that actually happened.
prompts.instruction: Correct the record. State what occurred without hedging or diplomatic padding.

prompts.story_starter: Some things don't require consensus.
prompts.instruction: Write from the position that certain decisions belong to one person. Make the case without arrogance.

prompts.story_starter: The meeting had been going in circles for forty minutes.
prompts.instruction: Write the intervention — the sentence that stops the spiral and points at what matters.

prompts.story_starter: She had spent three days writing the proposal.
prompts.instruction: Write the cover email that lets the work speak — confident framing, no over-explaining.

prompts.story_starter: The river had changed course while no one was watching.
prompts.instruction: Write the observation of an irreversible change — clear-eyed, not mournful.

prompts.story_starter: He had one shot at this.
prompts.instruction: Write the moment of maximum focus — not tension, but clarity. The calm before action.

prompts.story_starter: The instructions were inadequate.
prompts.instruction: Write the replacement instructions — clearer, more direct, useful.

prompts.story_starter: She knew the conversation would be difficult.
prompts.instruction: Write the opening of it — honest, compassionate, direct. Set the terms without flinching.

prompts.story_starter: The first version was always wrong.
prompts.instruction: Write the second version — the one that actually says what needs to be said.

prompts.story_starter: He had listened long enough.
prompts.instruction: Write the response that breaks the silence — measured, direct, overdue.

prompts.story_starter: There were three things she would not compromise on.
prompts.instruction: Name them. State why, briefly. Hold the line.

prompts.story_starter: The best way forward was obvious — to her.
prompts.instruction: Write the explanation that makes it obvious to others, without condescension.

prompts.story_starter: She had spent years being agreeable.
prompts.instruction: Write the turn — the moment the habit breaks. Specific, surprising, irreversible.

prompts.story_starter: The question of blame was irrelevant.
prompts.instruction: Write past blame toward solution — not dismissively, but with productive focus.

prompts.story_starter: The old way had worked for a long time.
prompts.instruction: Write the case for the new way — respectful of what came before, clear about why it's time.

prompts.story_starter: Nobody wanted to be the one to say it.
prompts.instruction: Be the one. Say it plainly. Then make the case.

prompts.story_starter: The email had gone unanswered for two weeks.
prompts.instruction: Write the follow-up — clear, un-apologetic, actionable.

prompts.story_starter: She had learned this the hard way.
prompts.instruction: State the lesson. Not as wisdom-dispensing, but as plain fact from plain experience.

prompts.story_starter: The time for deliberation had passed.
prompts.instruction: Write the action that follows — swift, certain, consequential.

prompts.story_starter: His opinion had not changed in thirty years.
prompts.instruction: Write the defence of it — not stubbornness, but principle held under real pressure.

prompts.story_starter: The room needed someone to take responsibility.
prompts.instruction: Write the moment someone does. Specific action, no fanfare.

prompts.story_starter: She had been underestimated for the last time.
prompts.instruction: Write what follows — not revenge, not resentment, but decisive demonstration.

prompts.story_starter: The terms were non-negotiable.
prompts.instruction: Write the explanation of why — fair, firm, final.

prompts.story_starter: After everything, this was what remained.
prompts.instruction: Write the conclusion — clear-eyed, accepting, ready for next. No looking back.

prompts.story_starter: She signed the document without hesitation.
prompts.instruction: Write what she was thinking at that moment — certain, at peace with the choice.

prompts.story_starter: The last thing she wanted was sympathy.
prompts.instruction: Write what she wanted instead — specific, earned, on her own terms.

prompts.story_starter: It seemed, at least at first, like the right decision.
prompts.instruction: Continue in a voice that holds the decision lightly — aware that first impressions can deceive.

prompts.story_starter: Perhaps the map had never been accurate.
prompts.instruction: Write from a position of useful uncertainty — exploring without claiming arrival.

prompts.story_starter: One might argue — and many have — that the problem was simpler than it appeared.
prompts.instruction: Continue the argument without ever fully committing to it. Hold multiple views simultaneously.

prompts.story_starter: He wasn't sure what he'd seen, but it had unsettled him.
prompts.instruction: Write into the unsettlement — not resolving it, letting it stay alive on the page.

prompts.story_starter: The evidence pointed in one direction, though not conclusively.
prompts.instruction: Write the careful analysis — acknowledging what it suggests without overstating.

prompts.story_starter: She thought she understood what had happened. Mostly.
prompts.instruction: Write into that 'mostly' — what remained uncertain and why it mattered.

prompts.story_starter: In retrospect — if retrospect could be trusted — the signs had been there.
prompts.instruction: Write the careful retrospection that doesn't claim more than memory allows.

prompts.story_starter: It was possible that she had misread the situation entirely.
prompts.instruction: Write the possibility out fully — not self-flagellation, but honest re-examination.

prompts.story_starter: The answer, if there was one, wasn't simple.
prompts.instruction: Write toward complexity — acknowledge the shape of the problem without forcing resolution.

prompts.story_starter: She left.
prompts.instruction: Continue in the fewest words possible. No explanation. Trust the gap.
prompts.word_guidance: Aim for 60–80 words. Every word essential.

prompts.story_starter: The light changed.
prompts.instruction: Write what follows — three or four sentences, each earning its place. Nothing decorative.

prompts.story_starter: He had one question.
prompts.instruction: Ask it. Then write the silence that follows. No answer needed.

prompts.story_starter: The window was open.
prompts.instruction: Write the scene in which this detail is the most important thing. No adverbs.

prompts.story_starter: Years passed.
prompts.instruction: Write what those years left behind — two or three concrete objects or facts. Nothing more.

prompts.story_starter: He didn't say anything.
prompts.instruction: Write what the silence communicated. Show through context, not explanation.

prompts.story_starter: The door was locked.
prompts.instruction: Write the whole story in one more paragraph. What it means. No backstory.

prompts.story_starter: She folded the letter.
prompts.instruction: Write the gesture completely — what it says without words. No interiority needed.

prompts.story_starter: Nothing left to say.
prompts.instruction: Say the nothing. Make the absence feel earned and full.

prompts.story_starter: Okay, so here's the thing —
prompts.instruction: Finish the thought the way you'd explain it to a friend over coffee. Natural, direct, warm.

prompts.story_starter: You know that feeling when something just doesn't add up?
prompts.instruction: Explore it conversationally — no thesis, just thinking out loud with a reader you trust.

prompts.story_starter: I've been thinking about this a lot lately.
prompts.instruction: Write as if continuing a conversation that's been ongoing. Assume shared context.

prompts.story_starter: The weird thing is, nobody talks about this.
prompts.instruction: Talk about it. In exactly the voice you'd use if you just brought it up at dinner.

prompts.story_starter: So I tried it. And — honestly? It was fine.
prompts.instruction: Continue the honest, slightly anticlimactic account. Conversational realism.

prompts.story_starter: There's no elegant way to say this.
prompts.instruction: Say it anyway, inelegantly, which is its own kind of elegance. Honest, unpolished, direct.

prompts.story_starter: The following analysis examines the primary factors contributing to the observed outcome.
prompts.instruction: Continue in formal register — complete sentences, precise terminology, structured argument.

prompts.story_starter: It is worth noting, at the outset, that the matter at hand requires careful consideration.
prompts.instruction: Write the careful consideration. One claim per sentence. Evidence before conclusion.

prompts.story_starter: The committee met to address a matter of some urgency.
prompts.instruction: Write the minutes-adjacent account — factual, structured, impersonal, complete.

prompts.story_starter: The harbour held the light differently in November.
prompts.instruction: Continue the image — let sound and rhythm carry as much meaning as the words themselves.
prompts.word_guidance: 80–110 words. Read aloud as you write.

prompts.story_starter: She kept her grief in a blue ceramic bowl.
prompts.instruction: Explore the metaphor. Don't explain it — deepen it, complicate it, let it breathe.

prompts.story_starter: The city was a sentence that hadn't been finished.
prompts.instruction: Write the rest of that sentence — and what it means to leave things incomplete.

prompts.story_starter: The pattern became visible only after the third data point.
prompts.instruction: Describe the pattern — its structure, its implications, what it does and doesn't prove.

prompts.story_starter: The cause was not what the initial evidence suggested.
prompts.instruction: Write the reanalysis — careful, step-by-step, distinguishing correlation from causation.

prompts.story_starter: To understand the outcome, one must first understand the mechanism.
prompts.instruction: Explain the mechanism. Use precision — technical terms are acceptable if necessary.

prompts.story_starter: What if the problem had never been the problem?
prompts.instruction: Write into the question — don't answer it, but explore what opens up when you hold it.

prompts.story_starter: She had been asking the wrong questions for years.
prompts.instruction: Write the moment of recognising this — and the new question that opens as a result.

prompts.story_starter: The door opened, and she had no idea what she was looking for.
prompts.instruction: Write the looking — curious, open, unframed by expectation.

prompts.story_starter: Three systems had failed simultaneously: the technical, the relational, and the epistemic.
prompts.instruction: Unpack all three in a single paragraph — compressed, layered, analytically precise.

prompts.story_starter: The decision was political, economic, and personal — in that order.
prompts.instruction: Write the paragraph that handles all three at once, without losing any.

prompts.story_starter: History doesn't repeat; it compounds.
prompts.instruction: Write the argument for this claim — dense, specific, using concrete examples as evidence.

prompts.story_starter: The summer she turned thirty-two, she began to notice things differently.
prompts.instruction: Write into the noticing — take time, follow threads, let the observation unfold without rushing to meaning.

prompts.story_starter: He had been thinking about the river for months.
prompts.instruction: Write the extended meditation — allow digressions, follow associations, return to the river changed.

prompts.story_starter: The argument had been going on for years before either of them noticed it was the same argument.
prompts.instruction: Write the gradual recognition — unhurried, layered, earning its revelation through accumulation.
